items.title: Archive of Byte magazine, starting with issue #1 in 1975
items.author: DamnInteresting
items.summary: Byte magazine began in 1975, focusing on personal computing, and ceased publication in 1998. Its first issue featured articles on early personal computers. Archive.org hosts its early issues for free access.

items.title: Notion leaks email addresses of all editors of any public page
items.author: Tiberium
items.summary: Public Notion pages leak email addresses of all editors. This vulnerability exposes personal information. This issue persists as of 2026-04-19.

items.title: Game devs explain the tricks involved with letting you pause a game
items.author: speckx
items.summary: To pause a game, developers often set the game’s timescale to 0, freezing time while allowing the UI to function. Some developers freeze time and use a screenshot as the pause menu background, enabling them to manipulate game elements behind the scenes. This method allows them to stop rendering enemies or relocate the player.

items.title: The seven programming ur-languages (2022)
items.author: helloplanets
items.summary: The seven programming ur-languages in 2022 are Algol 68, Scheme, Common Lisp, ML, Forth, Actor, and metaprogramming. These languages emphasize foundational concepts in programming.

items.title: Turtle WoW classic server announces shutdown after Blizzard wins injunction
items.author: Brajeshwar
items.summary: Turtle WoW, a popular private World of Warcraft Classic server, announced its shutdown after Blizzard obtained a legal injunction against it for copyright infringement. Blizzard's victory in the lawsuit led to the server's closure. This followed a similar pattern to the shutdown of Nostalrius.

items.title: Show HN: Google Gemini Is Scanning Your Photos – and the EU Said No
items.author: anju-kushwaha
items.text:
Google has expanded its Personal Intelligence feature so that Gemini can now access your Google Photos face data, Gmail, YouTube history, and search activity to generate personalized AI images — live for US paid subscribers as of April 2026.

https://vucense.com/privacy-sovereignty/surveillance-biometr
items.summary: Google Gemini scans users' photos and emails, raising privacy concerns; EU regulations oppose this practice; users' consent is often not obtained.

items.title: Pairwise Order of a Sequence of Elements
items.author: ibobev
items.summary: Pairwise order determines relative positions of elements; a unique sequence requires complete ordering constraints; algorithms can optimize order based on pairwise scores.

items.title: College instructor turns to typewriters to curb AI-written work
items.author: gnabgib
items.summary: A college instructor at Cornell University uses typewriters to reduce reliance on AI in student writing. This method encourages students to engage more deeply with their assignments. The approach aims to enhance traditional writing skills.

items.title: Show HN: Prompt-to-Excalidraw demo with Gemma 4 E2B in the browser (3.1GB)
items.author: teamchong
items.summary: A browser-based tool converts text prompts into Excalidraw diagrams using Gemma 4 E2B, requiring 3.1GB. It's available at trendstack-xi.vercel.app/trending-today.

items.title: The electromechanical angle computer inside the B-52 bomber's star tracker
items.author: NelsonMinar
items.summary: The electromechanical angle computer in the B-52 bomber's star tracker automatically tracked a single star to provide highly accurate navigation headings. It used a spiral search mechanism to locate the star and maintained automatic tracking. This system did not require manual star tracking by the navigator.

items.title: Minimal Viable Programs (2014)
items.author: bachmeier
items.summary: A minimal viable program is the smallest, simplest version of a software solution that addresses a problem. Joe Armstrong's 2014 article on the topic emphasizes its core functionality without extras. The concept remains relevant today.

items.title: Airline worker arrested after sharing photos of bomb damage in WhatsApp group
items.author: aa_is_op
items.summary: An airline worker was arrested in Dubai after sharing bomb damage photos on a private WhatsApp group, leading to police surveillance and his arrest. The incident highlights concerns over privacy and electronic surveillance.

items.title: Why Japan has such good railways
items.author: RickJWagner
items.summary: Japan's railways are efficient due to privatization, extensive network, and government support for profitable operations. The rail system benefits from modern, reliable trains and extensive station coverage. Japan's historical embrace of diversification in railway business models enhances its success.

items.title: The world in which IPv6 was a good design (2017)
items.author: signa11
items.summary: IPv6 was a good design in a world with no NAT and where every device had a unique global IP address. It aimed to provide a vast address space to eliminate IP address exhaustion. The design was intended to simplify routing and eliminate the need for complex NAT systems.

items.title: Matt Mullenweg Overrules Core Committers; Puts Akismet on WP 7's Connector List
items.author: mooreds
items.summary: Matt Mullenweg overrules core committers to include Akismet on WordPress 7.0's Connectors Screen. Some committers opposed the decision, but it was approved. Akismet will appear alongside other services on the Connectors Screen.

items.title: Modern Common Lisp with FSet
items.author: larve
items.summary: FSet is a functional collections library for Common Lisp, enhancing it with functional programming tools. It complements the language's focus on CLOS and imperative programming. The library aims to educate Lisp programmers in functional thinking.

items.title: Optimizing Ruby Path Methods
items.author: weaksauce
items.summary: To optimize Ruby path methods, avoid unnecessary stat calls and use Dir.glob instead of Dir.scan for faster performance. Use caching for repeated path lookups. Profiling and benchmarking are essential for identifying bottlenecks.

items.title: Spiral staircase with a single guardrail once led to the top of the Eiffel Tower
items.author: bookofjoe
items.summary: A spiral staircase with a single guardrail once led to the top of the Eiffel Tower, featuring 1,062 steps connecting the second and third floors. Although open to visitors, it was later restricted. A section of this original staircase is set to be auctioned by Artcurial in 2026.

items.title: Bypassing the kernel for 56ns cross-language IPC
items.author: riyaneel
items.summary: Tachyon achieves 56ns cross-language IPC by bypassing the kernel, significantly reducing latency compared to alternatives like eventfd. It avoids syscall overhead and uses efficient spin-waiting mechanisms. Performance is optimal under low contention.

items.title: Thoughts and feelings around Claude Design
items.author: cdrnsf
items.summary: Claude Design is an AI tool for creating visual designs. Users describe their vision, and the AI generates a first version. Users refine the design via chat, comments, text editing, and sliders. It was launched in April 2026.

items.title: Scientists discover “cleaner ants” that groom giant ants in Arizona desert
items.author: t-3
items.summary: Scientists found tiny cone ants grooming larger harvester ants in Arizona's desert. These cleaner ants may feed on particles removed from the harvester ants. This mutualistic relationship is rare in the insect world.
